segments.s484-g41: فرستاده (subject) + آمد (simple past 'came') followed by a prepositional phrase 'به درگه فراز' marking destination. In classical/poetic word order the adjective/epithet 'فراز' follows 'درگه' and qualifies it as 'on high/exalted'. The structure is S V + PP ('to the exalted court').
segments.s484-g42: بگفتند is the past, 3rd-person-plural ('they said' or impersonally 'it was said'); here it introduces an announcement. با + noun (با فور) forms an adverbial phrase of manner/tempo ('with immediacy' = 'at once'), and گردن‌فراز is a descriptive compound used adverbially ('with neck raised' = 'proudly'). Together they describe how the saying/announcement was made.
segments.s484-g43: جهاندیده را is the definite/direct object marked by the object particle را. پیش او is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning 'in his presence' or 'before him'. خواندند is past, 3rd-person-plural; here it means 'they called/summoned' (not 'read'). The order is Object + locative + Verb, a normal Persian arrangement.
segments.s484-g44: بر تخت نزدیک is a prepositional phrase: بر ('on/upon') + تخت ('throne') with the qualifier نزدیک ('near') following the noun (typical adjective position in classical Persian), yielding 'near the throne' or 'close to the throne'. بنشاندند is the past, 3rd-person-plural of نشاندن (causative 'to seat/settle'); it means 'they caused him to sit' / 'they seated him'.
segments.s484-g9: سر نامه کرد = ‘(he) made/wrote at the head (of the letter)’. سر here means ‘top / head (of the letter)’ and is followed by نامه (letter). کرد is the simple past ‘did/made’. آفرین خدای is an opening formula ‘praise to God’ (آفرین as an exclamation + خدای ‘God’) functioning as the phrase he put at the head.
segments.s484-g10: This clause is an invocation/optative: کجا بود و باشد همیشه به جای = literally ‘where he was and may he be always in place’. بود is past (‘was’); باشد is optative/subjunctive ‘may he remain/be’; همیشه به جای is a time/place phrase ‘always in his place’. The juxtaposition بود و باشد pairs past and jussive to wish continuance of a past state.
segments.s484-g11: کسی را که او کرد پیروزبخت = ‘the person whom he made fortunate’. را is the definite/object marker attaching to کسی; که introduces the relative clause; او کرد پیروزبخت is a verb–predicate sequence ‘he made (کرد) (پیروزبخت) fortunate’, with the predicate after the verb in this classical word order.
segments.s484-g12: بماند بدو کشور و تاج و تخت = ‘may (it) remain to him — the country, crown and throne’. بماند is here jussive/optative ‘may remain / may endure’, followed by the dative phrase بدو ‘to him’; the list کشور و تاج و تخت names what is wished to remain to him.
segments.s484-g13: گرش خوار گیرد بماند نژد is a compact conditional: گرش ‘its course/orbit’ + خوار ‘(a) despiser/mean one’ + گیرد ‘takes/seizes’ (present/future sense here) => ‘if the mean one seizes its course’. بماند نژد follows as the result clause ‘it remains near’. Word order is compressed and classical: condition then result.
segments.s484-g14: نتابد برو آفتاب بلند = ‘the lofty sun does not shine (forth)’. نتابد is the negative of تابیدن ‘to shine’ (here ‘does not/ will not shine’). آفتاب بلند ‘the high/lofty sun’ is the subject phrase; برو here is an incidental vocative/marker attached to the noun phrase in poetry (addressing or pointing to the sun).
segments.s484-g15: شنیدی همانا که یزدان پاک = a rhetorical opener ‘Have you heard, truly, that the pure God...’. شنیدی is second‑person past ‘you heard/have you heard’; همانا is an emphatic particle ‘indeed/truly’; که introduces the following content clause; یزدان پاک = ‘pure God’ (subject of the clause).
segments.s484-g16: چه دادست ما را بدین تیره خاک = ‘what (indeed) has given to us by/in this dark soil’. چه is exclamative ‘what’/‘how’; دادست is an archaic perfect/past ‘has given’ (classical form combining داد + است); ما را marks ‘to us’; بدین تیره خاک is a prepositional phrase ‘by/with this dark/ominous earth’ (بدین = به + این ‘by/with this’ + تیره خاک ‘dark soil/land’).
segments.s484-g25: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تخت بلندی is a noun + attribute meaning 'lofty throne' (adjective/attribute after the noun is normal in Persian). به introduces the goal ('onto/into') and اسپ is 'horse'. اندر آی is an older/poetic multiword command: اندر (into/on) + آی (imperative 'come!') = 'mount' or 'get onto'.
segments.s484-g26: مزن is a negative imperative 'do not ...' formed from زدن (here used idiomatically). رای is the object meaning 'counsel/plan/opinion' (i.e. 'take counsel'). با + موبد و رهنمای = 'with the mobed and the adviser'; با is the standard 'with' preposition and و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s484-g27: ز ما = 'from us' (seek something from us). ایمنی خواه is a terse verb phrase: ایمنی (safety) + خواه (want/seek) used as an imperative/request 'seek safety'. و links the next negative command: چاره مساز (چاره + مساز) where مساز is negative imperative 'do not make/contrive' (i.e. 'do not devise a scheme').
segments.s484-g28: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for/because'). بر is the preposition 'upon/for' governing چاره‌گر ('one who makes remedies' / 'solver'). کار گردد is a passive/impersonal turn meaning 'the task/work becomes' (گردد = 'become'), and دراز is the predicate adjective 'long' describing the resulting state.
segments.s484-g29: ز فرمان = 'from/by the command' (poetic preposition). اگر introduces a conditional clause; یک زمان 'a single moment' is the temporal adjunct. بگذری is the present/subjunctive form of گذشتن used in classical Persian after اگر to express a hypothetical 'if you pass/neglect (it) for a moment'.
segments.s484-g30: بلندی گزینی is a verb phrase where گزینی (second‑person singular present/subjunctive or future sense 'you will choose') takes بلندی ('height/exaltation') as its object — 'you will choose exaltation'. و connects the following noun کنداوری, here a proper name; the clause thus lists two things someone will choose. The verb form گزینی carries the prospective/result implication.
segments.s484-g31: چو = 'like/as'; آتش is a simile 'like fire'. سپاهی گران = 'a weighty/powerful army' (سپاهی 'army' + گران 'heavy/important'). بیارم in classical Persian would be a verb 'I will bring' (or 'I bring') but the supplied lemma senses did not include the verbal meaning here (so treated specially); syntactically it is the speaker's verb introducing what he will bring.
segments.s484-g32: گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/selected' modifying دلیران 'the brave ones/warriors'. کنداوران is the group/name that these adjectives qualify; in context it functions as a plural noun (a troop or people). The three words together form a noun phrase 'the chosen, brave Kandaurans'.
segments.s484-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (از). پیروزی 'victory' and بخت 'fortune' are coordinated by و 'and'. وز here is actually a fused form equivalent to 'و + ز' ('and from') linking the next item; in classical/poetic Persian writers often repeat the preposition. فرهی is used as a noun/adjective 'the noble' / 'nobility'. The whole phrase lists sources introduced by از/ز.
segments.s484-g18: The initial ز again means 'from'. دیهیم here refers to the people of the village / common folk (used as a source in the list). وز again functions as 'و + ز' ('and from') rather than the literal senses listed for وز, so no listed sense matches. تخت شاهنشهی is a noun phrase 'the imperial throne' (تخت 'throne' qualified by شاهنشهی 'imperial').
segments.s484-g19: نماند is the negated third-person singular verb 'does not remain' (will not last). همی is a classical/epic adverbial particle adding emphasis ('constantly/indeed' or intensifying the statement). روز ما 'our day' is the affected noun phrase. بگذرد is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'will pass' — here paired with نماند to say the day will not endure but will pass.
segments.s484-g20: کسی دیگر آید is 'someone else will come' (آید third-person singular present/subjunctive/indicative). کزو = 'from which / from whom' functions to introduce the relative/result clause. بر here is the prepositional/verb prefix 'upon' and خورد is the verb 'to befall / to happen' (بر خورد = 'to befall, to come upon'); together they mean 'will befall him / will take place for him' or 'who will receive it'.
segments.s484-g21: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'constantly/ever'. نام here means 'name' in the sense of reputation/fame. کوشم is first-person singular 'I strive/endeavor' in the classical present. که introduces a purpose/desiderative clause 'that ...'. ماند is third-person singular 'may remain / endure' (optative/indicative sense), and نه ننگ negates 'shame' — together 'that my name remain, not (be) shame.'
segments.s484-g22: بدین here is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'in/with this' (i.e. 'in this'). مرکز 'center' is the head noun; ماه و پرگار 'moon and compass' form a poetic, metaphorical coordination (an image of a small orbit or narrow circle), with تنگ 'narrow' qualifying the compound. The phrase describes the limited sphere in which the speaker wants his name to remain honorable.
segments.s484-g23: چو = 'when'. این نامه 'this letter' is the object. آرند is the classical third-person plural present 'they bring' (here translated passively as 'when this letter is brought'). نزدیک تو 'near you' is the adverbial/locative phrase indicating reception by the addressee.
segments.s484-g24: بی‌آزار 'harmless' is an adjective used as the goal/result. کن is the imperative 'do/make' addressed to 'you' (تو). رای تاریک تو is the noun phrase 'your dark counsel/plan' (تاریک modifies رای). The command asks the addressee to render his dark counsel ineffective/harmless; word order places the adjective/result before the imperative verb in this compact poetic phrasing.
segments.s484-g1: ز is the poetic form of از meaning "from/since," introducing a circumstantial phrase; میلاد here reads as a noun "birth." چون ... براند is a simile clause: چون ('like/as') + باد ('wind') precedes the verb براند (rāndan) in the third-person present/past-tense form (here narratively simple past/epic present) meaning "(he) drove/led." لشکر is the object/affected noun "army." The whole segment sets the time/circumstance: "from (his) birth, when he drove the army like the wind."
segments.s484-g2: به introduces a goal/direction "toward/into" (here motion toward a place). قنوج is the place-name Kannauj. شد (shod) is the common narrative verb meaning roughly "happened/arrived" (here used like "went/arrived"); it links the motion phrase to the following subject. گنجش is a poetic form of گنج "treasure," and آنجا بماند is a short clause with بماند (third-person present/subjunctive/epic past) meaning "remained/stayed"; together: "the treasure went to Kannauj and remained there."
segments.s484-g3: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." آورد is the past narrative form of آوردن "to bring/lead," so "(he) brought/led." لشکر is the direct object/subject in context "the army." به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase "to/near," and فور appears as a place-name here (not the adverbial sense); because the provided senses don't include this toponym, its sense is outside the listed options. Entire clause: "when he led the army near For."
segments.s484-g4: یکی before نامه is an indefinite / numeral "a certain/one," so "a certain letter." فرمود is the polite/narrative verb often used for "said/commanded/wrote/sent" — here it functions as "issued/sent (a letter)." پر is the prepositional adjective "full (of)" taking the following nouns as its content; جنگ و شور are the coordinating nouns "war and tumult," describing the letter's content or tone: "a letter full of war and tumult."
segments.s484-g5: ز again marks origin/agent "from/of." شاهنشه is the title "emperor" (poetic form of شاهنشاه) followed by the proper name اسکندر (Alexander) and then فیلقوس (a secondary name/epithet). The sequence is appositional/title + personal names: "from Emperor Alexander Filqūs."
segments.s484-g6: فروزنده is an active/agentive participle/adjective meaning "brightener/illuminator/kindler" placed before a coordinated list of direct nouns. آتش is "fire," نعم here is the poetic form for bounty/blessing (نِعمَت/نعمت), and بوس is the noun "kiss." The coordinating و link the nouns; the phrase functions as an epithet describing Alexander/Filqūs ("the brightener of fire and blessings and kisses").
segments.s484-g7: سوی is a directional preposition "toward(s)." فور here is a place-name (sense not given in the provided list). هندی is an adjective "Indian" modifying سپهدار (spahdār, military commander). هند is the genitive or appositional place-name "Hind/India," so the chunk reads as a titled person: "the Indian spahdār of Hind."
segments.s484-g8: This segment contains epithets: بلند اختر literally "lofty/high star" (a poetic honorific) coordinated with لشکر آرای which is a compound idea "army-adorner/arrayer" (لشکر + آرای[نده/آرا] used poetically). سند is the place-name Sind (the region) specifying which army he adorns. Together they form an honorific apposition describing the commander: "the lofty star and army-adorner of Sind."
segments.s484-g33: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” من is the subject “I.” باسواران is written as a single token but = با سواران (“with the riders/horsemen”), an accompanying prepositional phrase modifying the subject. بیایم is the present-subjunctive/relative form often used in classical Persian for temporal clauses and here = “I come/when I come.” به جنگ is a directional phrase “into/for battle.”
segments.s484-g34: پشیمانی is the noun ‘regret.’ آید is the impersonal/present verb ‘comes’ (here future/inevitable sense). ترا (ت + را) is the 2nd-person enclitic plus the object/emphatic marker (literally ‘to you’ / ‘upon you’). زین درنگ is an ablative/causal phrase ‘from/for this delay (in saddling)’ that gives the reason for the regret. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: subject (پشیمانی) + verb (آید) + recipient (ترا را) + cause (زین درنگ).
segments.s484-g35: چو again = “when.” زین باره is a compound meaning ‘on/with respect to this matter’ (زین = this, باره = occasion/case). گفتارها is the plural subject ‘the words/statements.’ سخته شد is a predicative adjective + شد meaning ‘became hard/difficult/harsh’ — i.e. the utterances became hard to speak or awkward.
segments.s484-g36: نویسنده = ‘the writer/author’ (subject). از + نامه here links the writer to the act concerning the letter (here best read as ‘in respect of the letter’ / ‘with the letter’). پردخته شد is a past/passive construction built on a participial/adjectival stem (پردخته) + شد, meaning roughly ‘ceased/withdrew from composing’ or more literally ‘was (no longer) composing’ — in context the sense is that the writer stops or refrains from composing the letter because the words became difficult.
segments.s484-g37: نهادند is the past plural verb ‘they placed/put.’ مهر سکندر is a genitive phrase ‘Alexander’s seal’ (مهر = seal). به روی is a locative phrase ‘upon the surface / on the face (of it)’ indicating where the seal was placed.
segments.s484-g38: بجستند here functions as a past action ‘they sought / they found’ (poetic compression). پید ا is the predicate/adjective ‘found / appeared.’ یکی نامجوی = ‘a certain one, a seeker-of-name’ (یکی = someone / a certain one; نامجوی = a person who seeks a name/renown). Together the clause says they discovered/encountered a certain seeker of renown.
segments.s484-g39: فرستاده شاهش is a genitive NP ‘the king’s messenger’ (فرستاده = messenger, شاهش = his king/the king). به نزدیک is an adverbial/locative phrase ‘to/nearby’; فور is an adverb ‘immediately/at once.’ The clause as a whole: ‘the king’s messenger came near immediately.’
segments.s484-g40: گهی ... گهی is a repetitive adverbial pattern meaning ‘now... now... / at times... at times....’ رزم گفتی: رزم ‘battle’ + گفتی here functions as ‘talk/speaking (of)’ or a verbal noun indicating speech about battle — i.e. ‘(he spoke of) battle.’ گهی بزم و سور: ‘at times (of) banquets and revelry’ (بزم = feast/banquet, سور = festivity). The verb of saying is elliptically supplied from the previous clause (‘he/they spoke’).

sentences.s484-s21: The messenger arrived at the lofty court; they announced it at once with a proud bearing.
sentences.s484-s22: They summoned the world-wise man before him and seated him close to the throne.
sentences.s484-s5: He began the letter with “Praise to God”; may He have been wherever, and may He ever remain in His place.
sentences.s484-s6: May the one whom he made fortunate keep for himself the realm, the crown, and the throne.
sentences.s484-s7: If a despiser seizes its course it will remain near; the lofty sun does not shine forth.
sentences.s484-s8: Have you heard, truly, what the pure God has given us in this dark soil?
sentences.s484-s13: Come down from the lofty throne and mount the horse; do not take counsel with the mobed and the adviser.
sentences.s484-s14: Ask safety from us and make no device/plan, for the work of a remedy‑maker becomes long.
sentences.s484-s15: If you neglect the command for even a single moment, you will choose exaltation and Kandauri.
sentences.s484-s16: I will bring, like fire, a powerful army — the chosen, brave Kandaurans.
sentences.s484-s9: From victory and fortune, and from nobility, from the village folk and from the imperial throne.
sentences.s484-s10: Our day will not last — it will pass; another person will come who will take (it) upon himself / to whom it will befall.
sentences.s484-s11: I strive that my name endure, not disgrace, in this narrow circle of moon and compass.
sentences.s484-s12: When they bring this letter near you, render your dark counsel harmless.
sentences.s484-s1: From his birth, when (he) drove the army like the wind, the treasure went to Kannauj and remained there.
sentences.s484-s2: When the army came near For, he sent a certain letter, full of war and tumult.
sentences.s484-s3: From Emperor Alexander, Filqūs, the brightener of fire and blessings and of kisses.
sentences.s484-s4: Toward For came the Indian spahdār of Hind, the lofty star and army-adorner of Sind.
sentences.s484-s17: When I come with the horsemen to battle, you will come to regret this delay in saddling.
sentences.s484-s18: When, on this matter, the words grew harsh, the writer ceased composing the letter.
sentences.s484-s19: They set Alexander’s seal upon the surface; they found a certain seeker of a name.
sentences.s484-s20: The king’s messenger came near at once; at times (he spoke of) battle, at times (of) feasting and revelry.
